Web Application: comprehensive-option-pricing-and-strategy-analyzer.streamlit.app

I’m open to suggestions! Kindly DM me on LinkedIn if you want any other features added or if you notice any mistakes that need correction.

title: Monte Carlo Credit Portfolio Risk Simulation: Multi-Factor Analysis of Tail Risk and Stress Amplification
display_title: Monte Carlo Credit Portfolio Risk Simulation: Multi-Factor Analysis of Tail Risk and Stress…
subtitle: GitHub Repository:https://github.com/NavnoorBawa/Monte-Carlo-Credit-Portfolio-Risk-Simulation
body_text:
Monte Carlo Credit Portfolio Risk Simulation: Multi-Factor Analysis of Tail Risk and Stress Amplification

GitHub Repository:https://github.com/NavnoorBawa/Monte-Carlo-Credit-Portfolio-Risk-Simulation

Abstract

This paper presents a Monte Carlo credit portfolio risk framework integrating empirical data from S&P Global, Moody’s Recovery Studies, Basel Framework parameters, and Federal Reserve stress tests. Our simulations reveal a significant tail risk that conventional analytical approaches systematically underestimate, with simulation-based VaR exceeding Vasicek analytical estimates by 37.5%. Furthermore, our stress testing methodology demonstrates dramatic capital requirement amplification (up to 1,529.9%) under adverse scenarios, highlighting the non-linear relationship between macroeconomic shocks and tail risk. Results demonstrate that asset correlation structures and wrong-way risk significantly amplify portfolio-level capital requirements beyond standard regulatory formulations.

1. Data Sources and Calibration

1.1 Default Probabilities

S&P Global Default Study (1981–2023):

AAA: 0.00%, AA: 0.02%, A: 0.05%, BBB: 0.14%, BB: 0.57%, B: 2.98%, CCC/C: 25.98%

1.2 Recovery Rates

Moody’s Annual Default Study:

First-lien recovery: 54.5% (Technology) to 73.9% (Beverage/Food)

Senior unsecured recovery: 26.4% (Telecommunications) to 46.3% (Automotive)

Stress period recovery: 7.8% lower than normal periods

1.3 Correlation Parameters

Basel II IRB Framework:

Asset correlation formula: R = 0.12×(1-EXP(-50×PD))/(1-EXP(-50))+0.24×[1-(1-EXP(-50×PD))/(1-EXP(-50))]

Corporate exposures: 0.12 (floor) to 0.24 (ceiling)

1.4 Stress Test Parameters

Federal Reserve DFAST/CCAR (2021–2024):

Severe adverse credit card loss rate: 17.6%

Severe adverse C&I loss rate: 8.1%

Severe adverse CRE loss rate: 8.8%

2. Methodology

2.1 Mathematical Framework

Our model implements the Merton-based asset value approach with multi-factor extensions:

Single-Factor Model:

Z_i = √ρ_i·Y + √(1-ρ_i)·ε_i
Default occurs when Z_i < Φ^(-1)(PD_i)

Multi-Factor Extension:

Z_i = Σ(a_ik·Y_k) + √(1-Σ(a_ik²))·ε_i

Wrong-Way Risk Implementation:

LGD_i = base_LGD_i + β_i·avg(Y) + ε_i^LGD
β_i = 0.1 (calibrated to crisis recovery patterns)
Negative systematic factors (Y < 0) increase LGD, aligning with empirical observations

2.2 Correlation Structure

Base correlation: 0.12

Industry add-on: 0.06

Rating similarity add-on: 0.04

Investment/non-investment grade adjustment: 0.85 multiplier

2.3 Numerical Implementation

SVD decomposition for correlation matrix stability

Gaussian kernel smoothing for robust VaR estimation

Beta distribution for stochastic LGD modeling

Eigenvalue correction to ensure positive definiteness

2.4 Stress Testing Methodology

We implement a multi-factor stress testing approach with:

PD multipliers (1.0× to 6.0×) calibrated to historical crises

LGD multipliers (1.0× to 1.6×) based on Moody’s recovery deterioration data

Correlation multipliers (1.0× to 1.5×) reflecting increased asset dependence

Factor-specific shocks to systematic risk factors configured with negative directionality to properly simulate adverse economic conditions

Stronger wrong-way risk effects (β = 0.25) during stress periods

3. Portfolio Characteristics

3.1 Exposure Profile

Total exposure: $45,897,358

Number of loans: 100

Weighted PD: 0.10%

Weighted LGD: 43.99%

3.2 Concentration Metrics

Name concentration (HHI): 0.0396

Industry concentration (HHI): 0.2171

Rating concentration (HHI): 0.2523

Top 10 exposure concentration: 53.84%

4. Key Results

4.1 Risk Metrics (Base Case)

Expected Loss (EL): $17,138

Value at Risk (VaR 99.9%): $626,809

Expected Shortfall (ES 99.9%): $942,434

Economic Capital (EC): $609,671

EC Ratio: 1.33%

4.2 Analytical vs. Simulation Comparison

Analytical EL: $20,372 (118.9% of simulated)

Analytical VaR: $455,837 (72.7% of simulated)

VaR difference: +37.5%

4.3 Stress Testing Results

Base Scenario EC Ratio: 4.04%

Moderate Recession EC Ratio: 65.31% (+1,518.4% capital)

Severe Recession EC Ratio: 65.78% (+1,529.9% capital)

Financial Crisis EC Ratio: 53.92% (+1,236.2% capital)

5. Empirical Validation

5.1 PD Calibration Results

Rating | Model PD | S&P Historical | Difference
-------|----------|---------------|------------
AAA    | 0.01%    | 0.01%         | +10.1%
AA     | 0.02%    | 0.02%         | +1.3%
A      | 0.05%    | 0.05%         | +2.3%
BBB    | 0.14%    | 0.14%         | +2.1%
BB     | 0.58%    | 0.57%         | +1.9%
B      | 2.92%    | 2.98%         | -2.0%
CCC/C  | 28.63%   | 25.98%        | +10.2%

5.2 Default Behavior

Portfolio default rate: 1.89%

Default amplification factor: 18.9× weighted PD

6. Key Findings

6.1 Tail Risk Underestimation

The analytical Vasicek formula underestimates VaR by 37.5%, indicating fatter tails than standard models predict

Correlation structures more complex than uniformly assumed in single-factor analytical models

Wrong-way risk between systematic factors and recovery rates amplifies tail losses

6.2 Stress Amplification Effects

Dramatic non-linear increases in capital requirements under stress (up to 1,529.9% in severe recession)

Expected Loss grows substantially, but Value-at-Risk grows proportionally more, widening the capital gap

Multi-factor stress dynamics create more realistic tail behavior than simplified approaches

6.3 Portfolio Effects

Default clustering creates a higher portfolio default rate (1.89%) than weighted PD (0.10%) would suggest

Portfolio concentration (HHI = 0.2171) contributes to tail risk beyond expected loss

Industry and rating concentrations contribute substantially to capital requirements

7. Technical Insights

7.1 Correlation Matrix Stability

Eigenvalue correction essential for simulation stability

Industry-specific correlations significantly impact default clustering

The multi-factor model provides more realistic sector-specific stress impacts

7.2 LGD Modeling

Beta distribution captures empirical recovery rate variability

Systematic factor dependence (β = 0.25) aligns with observed crisis recovery patterns

Wrong-way risk critical for capturing realistic stress losses

7.3 Stress Testing Dynamics

Negative factor shock directionality essential for proper stress representation

Factor-specific shocks are more effective than uniform, systematic shifts

LGD-systematic factor alignment critical for realistic capital amplification

Independent per-scenario seeds essential for robust estimation

8. Implications

8.1 Capital Adequacy

Standard approaches may underestimate tail risk by ~37.5%

Stress capital requirements are dramatically higher than base case (>1,500%)

Full economic cycle capital planning requires sophisticated stress modeling

8.2 Risk Management

Multi-factor approach better captures sector-specific vulnerabilities

Wrong-way risk substantially amplifies losses beyond independent models

Concentration risk magnifies capital impact beyond pure rating-based approaches

8.3 Regulatory Framework

Basel analytical formulas may not adequately represent tail risks

Stress testing methodologies should integrate:

Stronger factor-specific shocks

LGD-default correlation effects

Proper directional alignment of systematic effects

9. References

S&P Global (2023). Annual Global Corporate Default Study

Moody’s (2024). Annual Default Study: Corporate Default and Recovery Rates

BIS (2005). An Explanatory Note on the Basel II IRB Risk Weight Functions

Federal Reserve (2024). Dodd-Frank Act Stress Test Results

Repository

https://github.com/NavnoorBawa/Monte-Carlo-Credit-Portfolio-Risk-Simulation

Contact

For detailed discussions on the implementation or suggestions: Navnoor Bawa [LinkedIn Profile]

Note: This represents ongoing work, and I welcome feedback and suggestions for improvement.

title: Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Solving the Sharpe Ratio Paradox
display_title: Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Solving the Sharpe Ratio Paradox
subtitle: Our fourth iteration of statistical arbitrage implementation presents a mathematical anomaly worth examining. Using the Russell 3000 index, our implementation has achieved a 15.15% total return (34.49% annualized) with a negative Sharpe ratio of -0.14. This article analyzes this paradox and outlines our solution approach.
body_text:
Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Solving the Sharpe Ratio Paradox

Implementation Results & The Sharpe Ratio Challenge

Our fourth iteration of statistical arbitrage implementation presents a mathematical anomaly worth examining. Using the Russell 3000 index, our implementation has achieved a 15.15% total return (34.49% annualized) with a negative Sharpe ratio of -0.14. This article analyzes this paradox and outlines our solution approach.

Data and Methodology Overview

For context, our implementation:

Data Universe: Russell 3000 stocks (daily price and volume data)

Training Period: January 2019 — December 2022

Testing Period: January 2023 — December 2024 (out-of-sample)

Methodology: Statistical arbitrage pairs trading using Deep RL

Agents: Ensemble of DDPG and SAC models trained on the training dataset

Initial Capital: $1,000,000

Current Performance Metrics

Recent Implementation Enhancements

1. Enhanced Position Management

# Adaptive position sizing with multiple factors
position_size = (
    base_size * 
    zscore_factor * 
    half_life_factor * 
    vol_factor * 
    stress_factor * 
    correlation_factor * 
    sector_factor
)

This multifactor approach dynamically adjusts position sizes based on:

Z-score magnitude (more extensive for more significant deviations)

Mean reversion half-life (smaller for longer half-lives)

Pair volatility (reduced in high volatility regimes)

Market stress levels (reduced during stressed markets)

Correlation exposure (diversification-aware)

Sector concentration (sector neutrality)

2. Improved Exit Criteria

# Enhanced exit criteria examples
if pnl_pct < adjusted_stop_loss:
    positions_to_close.append((pair, "STOP_LOSS"))
    
if pnl_pct > adjusted_take_profit:
    positions_to_close.append((pair, "TAKE_PROFIT"))
    
if reversion_pct > 0.8 and pnl > 0:
    positions_to_close.append((pair, "MEAN_REVERSION_COMPLETE"))

Key improvements include:

Dynamic stop-loss thresholds based on pair volatility

Adaptive take-profit levels based on signal strength

Mean reversion completion detection

Signal reversal recognition

Time-based exits adjusted for half-life estimates

3. Optimized Agent Ensemble

# Improved combined position sizing
weighted_action = ddpg_weight * ddpg_action + (1 - ddpg_weight) * sac_action
weighted_action = 0.7 * weighted_action + 0.3 * mean_reversion_signal
agreement_factor = 0.4 + 0.6 * agreement
scaled_action = weighted_action * agreement_factor

Notable changes:

Enhanced agent weighting based on historical performance

Stronger integration of mean reversion signals (30% weight)

More lenient agreement scaling

Signal amplification for legitimate but weak signals

The Sharpe Ratio Paradox Explained

Despite strong absolute returns (15.15%), our strategy exhibits a negative Sharpe ratio (-0.14). This apparent contradiction stems from:

Calculation Mechanics: The Sharpe ratio compares daily returns to the risk-free rate:

excess_returns = returns - 0.02/252  # Daily risk-free rate
mean_excess = np.mean(excess_returns)
sharpe = (mean_excess / volatility) * np.sqrt(252)

2. Return Distribution: Our strategy displays positive skewness with:

Many small, frequent losses (win rate of 40.62%)

Occasional significant gains that drive overall profitability

Mean daily return below the daily risk-free rate despite positive cumulative returns

3. Volatility Impact: Low day-to-day volatility (1.00%) combined with thin average daily excess returns produces a negative Sharpe despite strong cumulative performance.

This pattern is characteristic of statistical arbitrage strategies with a positive skew, which is profitable overall while having suboptimal day-to-day risk-adjusted performance.

Next Steps: Addressing the Sharpe Ratio Challenge

1. Return Distribution Optimization

# Planned improvements for daily return distribution
def _calculate_entry_opportunities(self, spreads, current_data):
    # Filter for higher probability setups with daily return focus
    for pair, spread in spreads.items():
        # Enhanced probability of daily gains
        daily_edge = self._calculate_intraday_edge(spread, current_data)
        if daily_edge > min_daily_edge_threshold:
            entry_opportunities.append((pair, daily_edge))

2. Alternative Risk-Adjusted Metrics Implementation

def calculate_sortino_ratio(returns):
    """Calculate Sortino ratio using only downside deviations."""
    excess_returns = returns - (0.02/252)  # Daily risk-free rate
    downside_returns = excess_returns[excess_returns < 0]
    downside_deviation = np.std(downside_returns) * np.sqrt(252)
    return np.mean(excess_returns) * np.sqrt(252) / downside_deviation

This will better the strategy's actual risk-adjusted performance by:

Focusing on downside risk only (not penalizing upside volatility)

Better handling the positive skew characteristics

Providing a more accurate measure for strategies with asymmetric return distributions

3. Portfolio Balancing Enhancements

# Dynamic position count optimization
optimal_position_count = max(
    10,
    int(20 - (market_stress * 10) + (sharpe_trend * 5))
)

# Position concentration limits
allocation_per_position = min(
    0.03 * (1 + sharpe_trend),  # Base allocation
    0.05  # Maximum cap
)

Conclusion

Our Russell 3000 statistical arbitrage implementation demonstrates that positive absolute returns don’t necessarily translate to positive risk-adjusted performance metrics. This phenomenon is mathematically consistent and presents both a challenge and an opportunity for strategy refinement.

The negative Sharpe ratio, despite strong returns, highlights the importance of examining the entire return distribution, not just cumulative performance. Our next iteration will focus specifically on improving the consistency of daily returns while maintaining strong absolute performance.

Contact: For discussions or suggestions, please reach out to Navnoor Bawa [LinkedIn Profile]

Note: Performance metrics shown are from out-of-sample testing (Jan 2023 — Dec 2024).

title: Building a Comprehensive Options Pricing and Analysis Platform for Quantitative Research
display_title: Building a Comprehensive Options Pricing and Analysis Platform for Quantitative Research
subtitle: Options pricing and analysis sit at the intersection of stochastic calculus, numerical methods, and financial theory. As a quantitative researcher and financial engineer, I required a tool that precisely implements these models and provides the analytical capabilities to derive actionable insights. This article details the development of an open-source options analysis platform I’ve built from first principles with mathematical rigor at its core.
body_text:
Building a Comprehensive Options Pricing and Analysis Platform for Quantitative Research

Introduction: The Mathematical Foundations of Options

Options pricing and analysis sit at the intersection of stochastic calculus, numerical methods, and financial theory. As a quantitative researcher and financial engineer, I required a tool that precisely implements these models and provides the analytical capabilities to derive actionable insights. This article details the development of an open-source options analysis platform I’ve built from first principles with mathematical rigor at its core.

GitHub Repository: https://github.com/NavnoorBawa/options-pricing
 Web Application: https://comprehensive-option-pricing-and-strategy-analyzer.streamlit.app/

Theoretical Models: Ensuring Mathematical Precision

My implementation begins with three foundational pricing models, each meticulously implemented according to their theoretical specifications:

1. Black-Scholes-Merton Model

At the heart of options pricing lies the Black-Scholes-Merton differential equation:

∂V/∂t + (1/2)σ²S²(∂²V/∂S²) + rS(∂V/∂S) — rV = 0

For European options, I implement the closed-form solution:

C = S·N(d₁) — Ke^(-rT)·N(d₂)
 P = Ke^(-rT)·N(-d₂) — S·N(-d₁)

Where:
 d₁ = [ln(S/K) + (r + σ²/2)T]/(σ√T)
 d₂ = d₁ — σ√T

My implementation strictly enforces the put-call parity relationship:

C — P = S — Ke^(-rT)

The model includes comprehensive validation with rigorous test cases to ensure numerical accuracy within machine precision.

2. Cox-Ross-Rubinstein Binomial Model

For American options and more complex exercise structures, I implement the binomial tree model with parameters:

Up-factor: u = e^(σ√Δt)
 Down-factor: d = 1/u
 Risk-neutral probability: p = [e^(rΔt)-d]/(u-d)

My numerical optimization ensures convergence to the Black-Scholes solution as the number of steps increases, with verified test cases demonstrating the preservation of early exercise premium.

3. Monte Carlo Simulation

For complex path-dependent options and additional statistical insights, my Monte Carlo implementation uses:

S_t = S₀e^[(r-σ²/2)t+σW_t]

The platform provides variance reduction techniques and error estimation to achieve the accuracy required for research applications.

Greeks: The Complete Differential Structure

I’ve calculated the full spectrum of Greeks with analytical formulas rather than finite difference approximations where possible:

First-Order Greeks:
 Delta (Δ) = ∂V/∂S
 Gamma (Γ) = ∂²V/∂S²
 Theta (Θ) = ∂V/∂t
 Vega (ν) = ∂V/∂σ

Higher-Order Greeks:
 Vanna = ∂²V/∂S∂σ
 Charm = ∂²V/∂S∂t
 Volga = ∂²V/∂σ²
 Veta = ∂²V/∂σ∂t
 Speed = ∂³V/∂S³
 Zomma = ∂³V/∂S²∂σ
 Color = ∂³V/∂S²∂t
 Ultima = ∂³V/∂σ³

This allows researchers to analyze sensitivity to all market parameters at any order, enabling sophisticated hedging and model validation.

Strategy Implementation: Exact Payoff Functions

Beyond simple pricing, I’ve implemented a comprehensive suite of option strategies with mathematically precise payoff functions. For each strategy, I use the exact closed-form solutions rather than approximations:

For example, the Bull Call Spread has the precise payoff function:
 Payoff = max(0, S_T — K₁) — max(0, S_T — K₂) — Net Debit

And the Iron Condor implements:
 Payoff = Net Credit — max(0, K_put — S_T) + max(0, K_put-long — S_T) — max(0, S_T — K_call) + max(0, S_T — K_call-long)

Each strategy includes break-even analysis, maximum profit/loss calculations, and probabilistic profitability estimates using the underlying risk-neutral measure.

Advanced Volatility Modeling

For sophisticated volatility analysis, I’ve implemented:

Local Volatility Surface (Dupire’s Formula)

The full Dupire formulation:
 σ²_local(T,K) = [∂C/∂T + rK·∂C/∂K]/(0.5·K²·∂²C/∂K²)

This allows the extraction of local volatility from market data and creates a consistent volatility surface.

Implied Volatility Solver

Using the Newton-Raphson method for efficient implied volatility calculation:
 σ_n+1 = σ_n — f(σ_n)/f’(σ_n)
 where f(σ) = BS(S, K, T, r, σ) — Market Price

These tools enable sophisticated volatility research, which includes skew analysis, term structure modeling, and arbitrage detection.

[IMAGE SUGGESTION: 3D surface plot of a local volatility surface showing the volatility smile/skew across various strikes and expirations]

Quantitative Risk Management

The platform includes advanced risk management capabilities:

Value at Risk (VaR)

Monte Carlo VaR implementation with Expected Shortfall (ES) calculation:
 VaR_α(X) = inf{x: P(X ≤ x) > α}
 ES_α = E[X | X ≤ VaR_α]

Complete with distribution moment analysis (skewness, kurtosis) and statistical confidence intervals.

Portfolio Optimization

Multi-objective optimization for Greek-constrained portfolios:
 min_w Σ_i w_i·Objective_i
 subject to constraints on total delta, gamma, vega, and theta exposures.

Scenario Analysis

Stress testing framework with comprehensive market scenarios, including correlation modeling and volatility regime switches.

Research Applications

The platform is particularly valuable for several quantitative research applications:

Model Validation: Compare theoretical vs. market prices to identify model misspecification

Volatility Surface Analysis: Research volatility dynamics across strikes and expirations

Strategy Optimization: Backtest and optimize strategies with precise risk constraints

Greek Management: Study the evolution of sensitivities under various market conditions

Pricing Anomalies: Detect and quantify arbitrage opportunities across models and markets

Technical Implementation and Performance Considerations

My implementation prioritizes numerical stability and performance:

Vectorized calculations for high-performance Greek calculations

Variance reduction techniques for Monte Carlo

Optimized tree structure for binomial/trinomial models

Analytical formulas are favored over numerical approximations when available

Comprehensive validation suite with rigorous test cases

The platform scales well for portfolio analysis and maintains precision even with extreme parameter values.

GitHub Repository: https://github.com/NavnoorBawa/options-pricing
 Web Application: https://comprehensive-option-pricing-and-strategy-analyzer.streamlit.app/

I’m open to suggestions! Kindly DM me on LinkedIn if you want any other features added or if you notice any mistakes that need correction.

title: Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Enhanced Architecture and Risk Management
display_title: Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Enhanced Architecture and Risk Management
subtitle: This third iteration of my statistical arbitrage implementation combines sophisticated agent architectures with enhanced risk controls, leading to distinct performance characteristics. The implementation represents a significant evolution in addressing practical challenges in statistical arbitrage trading.
body_text:
Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Enhanced Architecture and Risk Management

This third iteration of my statistical arbitrage implementation combines sophisticated agent architectures with enhanced risk controls, leading to distinct performance characteristics. The implementation represents a significant evolution in addressing practical challenges in statistical arbitrage trading.

Performance Overview:

Core Architecture Strengths

The implementation leverages complementary strengths of DDPG and SAC agents:

DDPG Strengths:

class DDPGAgent:
    def __init__(self, state_dim, action_dim, hidden_dim=512):
        # Twin critics with gradient penalty
        self.critic = Critic(state_dim, action_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.critic2 = Critic(state_dim, action_dim, hidden_dim)
        # Adaptive exploration
        self.noise = OUNoise(action_dim, scale=0.8)
        self.noise_decay = 0.997

Twin delayed critics reduce overestimation bias

Adaptive noise decay enhances exploration

Wasserstein gradient penalty improves stability

2. SAC Benefits:

# Enhanced uncertainty estimation
q_uncertainty = torch.abs(next_q1 - next_q2)
base_risk_factor = torch.sigmoid(q_uncertainty)
next_q = next_q * (1 - risk_factor)  # Risk-adjusted values

Automatic entropy tuning

Uncertainty-based action scaling

Risk-factor modulated Q-values

Critical Architecture Changes

Volume-Aware Execution Infrastructure:

def calculate_market_impact(self, position_size, adv):
    trade_ratio = position_size / adv
    if trade_ratio <= 0.01:      # ≤1% ADV
        return 0.0007            # 7 bps
    elif trade_ratio <= 0.05:    # 1-5% ADV
        return 0.0015            # 15 bps
    return 0.0025               # >5% ADV

This granular impact model significantly improved execution realism, leading to:

Reduced trade frequency (31 total trades)

Higher per-trade profitability

More accurate transaction cost modeling

2. Enhanced Risk Assessment:

def calculate_monte_carlo_var(self, returns, positions):
    # GMM-based risk simulation
    self.gmm_risk_manager.fit(returns)
    simulated_returns = self.gmm_risk_manager.simulate(10000)
    portfolio_returns = np.dot(simulated_returns, weights)
    var = -np.percentile(portfolio_returns, 5)  # 95% VaR

Key improvements:

Better tail risk capture through GMM modeling

Regime-aware risk assessment

Enhanced drawdown control

3. Multi-Factor Position Sizing:

position_size = (
    base_size * 
    (1 - uncertainty) * 
    vol_scale * 
    trend_strength * 
    (1 - stress_level)
)

Key Infrastructure Enhancements

Advanced Position Management:

def _calculate_adv(self, pair, current_data):
    # Volume-aware position sizing with regime detection
    adv1 = vol1 * price1 * (1 - illiquidity_factor)
    adv2 = vol2 * price2 * (1 - illiquidity_factor)
    return min(adv1, adv2) * market_stress_adjustment

Dynamic ADV constraints

Liquidity-adjusted sizing

Stress-level position caps

2. Market Regime Integration:

def _calculate_regime_features(self, current_data, pair):
    # Multi-factor regime detection
    vol_regime = self._calculate_volatility_regime()
    corr_regime = self._calculate_correlation_regime()
    trend_regime = self._calculate_trend_regime()
    stress_level = self._calculate_market_stress()

Real-time regime classification

Adaptive risk thresholds

Enhanced signal filtering

Impact Analysis

Agent Behavior:

Combined DDPG/SAC predictions improve robustness

Enhanced exploration through dual approaches

Better uncertainty quantification

2. Risk Management:

More selective trading (31 total trades)

Higher but controlled volatility (44.96%)

Improved impact cost modeling

Challenges & Limitations

Volatility Management Issues:

# Current position sizing leads to high volatility
position_size = base_size * (1 - uncertainty) * vol_scale * trend_strength * (1 - stress_level)
# Results in:
# - 44.96% annualized volatility 
# - 12.69% maximum drawdow

Core Issue: The multiplicative nature of our position sizing factors can sometimes lead to oversized positions when multiple factors align, even with individual constraints.

2. Signal Generation Inefficiencies:

def kalman_filter_spread(self, pair, current_data):
    # Current Kalman implementation struggles with regime shifts
    kf = KalmanFilter(
        n_dim_obs=1,
        n_dim_state=2,
        initial_state_mean=np.zeros(2),
        initial_state_covariance=np.eye(2)
    )

The Kalman filter parameters remain static and don’t adapt quickly enough to regime changes, leading to delayed signal generation.

3. Trade Execution Challenges:

Low trade count (31 trades) suggests overly conservative entry criteria

Average holding period of 4.2 days may be too long given the high volatility

Market impact model may be too simplistic for larger positions

4. Risk Management Limitations:

# Current VaR calculation may underestimate tail risks
var = -np.percentile(portfolio_returns, 5)  # 95% VaR

GMM-based VaR doesn’t fully capture regime-dependent risk characteristics

Stop-loss mechanisms don’t account for liquidity conditions

Risk limits don’t dynamically adjust to market stress levels

5. Agent Architecture Issues:

# Current noise decay may be too aggressive
self.noise_decay = 0.997
self.noise_min = 0.1

# SAC temperature parameter needs better adaptation
self.target_entropy = -action_dim*2  # May not be optimal for all regimes

Exploration-exploitation balance needs better calibration

Agent ensemble doesn’t effectively handle regime transitions

Reward function may not properly penalize excessive risk-taking

Technical Updates Coming Soon

I will be posting detailed performance metrics and architecture improvements as the model completes its optimization iterations. The goal is to achieve stable, realistic performance while maintaining robust risk management.

Contact For detailed discussions on the implementation or suggestions: Navnoor Bawa [LinkedIn Profile]

Note: This represents ongoing work, and I welcome feedback and suggestions for improvement.

title: Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Addressing Overfitting Through Model Restructuring
display_title: Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Addressing Overfitting Through Model Restructuring
subtitle: Key Architecture Improvements & Performance Impact
body_text:
Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Addressing Overfitting Through Model Restructuring

Key Architecture Improvements & Performance Impact

In my previous implementation of the statistical arbitrage model using deep reinforcement learning, I encountered significant overfitting issues that resulted in unrealistic performance metrics (97.59% win rate). After thorough analysis and restructuring, I’ve made several critical improvements to create a more robust and realistic framework.

1. Training-Testing Data Separation

The most crucial improvement addresses the fundamental flaw in the previous implementation — the lack of proper separation between training and testing periods:

# Proper time period separation
training_start = '2019-01-01'
training_end = '2023-12-31'
testing_start = '2024-01-01' 
testing_end = '2025-01-01'

# Split data into distinct sets
training_data = processor.data[training_start:training_end]
testing_data = processor.data[testing_start:testing_end]

This separation prevents look-ahead bias and provides a more realistic assessment of model performance.

2. Enhanced Agent Architectures

DDPG Agent Improvements:

Implemented twin delayed critics for more stable learning

Added gradient penalty and Huber loss for robustness

Enhanced noise decay mechanism for better exploration

Implemented adaptive learning rates

# Enhanced DDPG critic loss with gradient penalty
critic_loss1 = F.mse_loss(current_Q1, target_Q) + 10.0 * grad_penalty1
critic_loss2 = F.mse_loss(current_Q2, target_Q) + 10.0 * grad_penalty2

SAC Agent Improvements:

Risk-aware policy improvement

Uncertainty-based exploration

Adaptive temperature optimization

Enhanced replay buffer prioritization

3. Current Performance Metrics

The current implementation shows more realistic, though initially lower, performance metrics:

While these results are significantly lower than the previous implementation, they represent a more realistic starting point for further optimization.

4. Ongoing Improvements

I am currently implementing several significant enhancements:

Market Regime-Aware Position Sizing:

def _calculate_regime_features(self, current_data, pair):
    """Calculate regime features for adaptive position sizing"""
    vol_regime = self._calculate_volatility_regime()
    corr_regime = self._calculate_correlation_regime()
    trend_regime = self._calculate_trend_regime()

2. Enhanced State Representation:

Extended state vector to 104 dimensions incorporating regime information

Added market stress indicators and uncertainty measures

Improved spread feature engineering

3. Advanced Risk Management Framework:

class PortfolioRiskManager:
    def __init__(self):
        self.confidence_level = 0.95
        self.stress_window = 21
        self.var_limit = 0.5  # 50% VaR limit
        self.max_drawdown = 0.15  # 15% max drawdown

4. Adaptive Trading Logic:

def generate_signals(self, pair, current_data, volume_data):
    # Regime-dependent thresholds
    entry_mult = 1 + abs(vol_regime) * 0.5
    base_entry = self.entry_threshold * entry_mult

Current Challenges & Next Steps

The implementation currently faces specific technical challenges:

Signal Generation:

Current signal hit rate: ~15–20%

Working on enhancing entry/exit criteria with regime filters

Implementing adaptive thresholds based on market conditions

2. Position Sizing:

Current max position size: 15% of capital

Developing dynamic sizing based on uncertainty metrics

Integrating correlation-based position limits

3. Risk Management:

Implementing rolling VaR calculations

Enhanced drawdown controls

Regime-dependent stop-loss levels

Technical Updates Coming Soon

I will be posting detailed performance metrics and architecture improvements as the model completes its optimization iterations. The goal is to achieve stable, realistic performance while maintaining robust risk management.

Contact For detailed discussions on the implementation or suggestions: Navnoor Bawa [LinkedIn Profile]

Note: This represents ongoing work, and I welcome feedback and suggestions for improvement.

title: Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Implementation Analysis & Current Challenges
display_title: Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Implementation Analysis & Current Challenges
subtitle: I have combined traditional statistical arbitrage with deep reinforcement learning (RL) techniques to build the current framework.
body_text:
Statistical Arbitrage with Deep RL: Implementation Analysis & Current Challenges

Model Performance

Current Implementation Analysis

Core Architecture

I have combined traditional statistical arbitrage with deep reinforcement learning (RL) techniques to build the current framework.

State Space Design (104 Dimensions)

The state space is designed with key market features as follows:

state = np.concatenate([
    hist1_norm,           # 48 dims: Price history 1
    hist2_norm,           # 48 dims: Price history 2
    [position],           # Current position
    [correlation],        # Rolling correlation
    [vol1, vol2],         # Individual volatilities
    [spread_zscore],      # Normalized spread
    [current_spread],     # Raw spread
    [spread_mean, spread_std]  # Spread statistics
])

Transaction Cost Model

I incorporate realistic institutional trading costs into the model:

Bid-ask spread: 15bps

Brokerage commission: 10bps

Market impact: 8bps

Exchange/regulatory fees: 2bps

This results in a 35bps per round trip.

Critical Issues Under Investigation

1. Win Rate Anomaly

The model’s 97.59% win rate is unusually high for statistical arbitrage strategies, and I am investigating it. Key focus areas include:

Assumptions around trade execution

Potential for look-ahead bias

Cross-validation methodology

2. Risk Management Framework

Risk management remains essential, and I have structured the current framework as follows:

# Current implementation
if position_size > max_position_limit:  # 25% cap
    position_size = max_position_limit

if total_exposure > max_exposure:  # 200% gross
    skip_trade = True

I am reviewing this structure to ensure its robustness in real-world trading conditions.

3. Market Impact Model

The market impact model is being refined to better reflect actual trading scenarios. The current version uses a nonlinear formula based on position size:

impact = base_impact * math.pow(position_size/adv, 0.6)
total_cost = bid_ask + commission + impact * (1 + time_decay)

Agents Implementation

1. DDPG Agent

Key characteristics of the DDPG agent I have implemented:

48-day lookback window

Deterministic position sizing (-100% to +100%)

Twin critics architecture for enhanced stability

2. SAC Agent

Key characteristics of the SAC agent:

Probabilistic position sizing

Built-in exploration via entropy maximization

Adaptive temperature parameter

References & Theoretical Framework

Primary Sources

Vidyamurthy, G. (2004). Pairs Trading: Quantitative Methods and Analysis

Gnabeyeu, E., Karkar, O., & Idboufous, I. (2024). Solving The Dynamic Volatility Fitting Problem: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach

Implementation Framework

Cointegration testing methodology from Vidyamurthy

Deep RL structure derived from Solving The Dynamic Volatility Fitting Problem: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach

Current Focus

I am currently focused on resolving the win rate anomaly and improving the transaction cost model. These statistical irregularities need to be resolved before deploying the model.

Technical Contact

For detailed discussions on statistical arbitrage, you can reach out to:

Navnoor Bawa
 LinkedIn Profile

Note: This is an ongoing project. I am focusing on the identified statistical anomalies before proceeding with deployment. I am open for any help or suggestions.
